bank: Headline with a question format gets more clicks than a declarative headline.
Headline using numbers or lists gets more clicks than a headline without numbers.
Headline incorporating emotional or sensational words gets more clicks than a neutral headline.
Headline that promises practical tips or solutions gets more clicks than a headline that only states facts.
Headline with shorter length (fewer words) gets more clicks than a longer, more complex headline.
Headline using first-person perspective (I, we) gets more clicks than third-person perspective.
Headline featuring a celebrity or well-known figure’s name gets more clicks than one without.
Headline with a sense of urgency or exclusivity (e.g., 'now', 'breaking') gets more clicks than a neutral time frame.
Headline employing humor or wordplay gets more clicks than a straightforward headline.
Headline that includes a direct call-to-action (e.g., 'You need to see this') gets more clicks than one without.
Headline with a positive sentiment gets more clicks than one with a negative sentiment.
Headline that uses active voice gets more clicks than one using passive voice.
Headline that includes a geographic location relevant to the audience gets more clicks than one without location.
Headline beginning with a strong keyword or theme word gets more clicks than one that starts with filler words.
Headline that hints at a mystery or incomplete information gets more clicks than a fully explicit headline.
